In general, here is what I would recommend about this:
- We use `///<` only for `public` definitions (we can also skip it if the constant name is descriptive enough and comment does not add much value.). 
- We use `///<` only when the entire description fits in same line, otherwise it's better to have a comment before the definition using `/** */` style.
- For `private` definitions, adding comment/docs is fully optional (up to developer), but we should not use `///<` so it won't be added in doxygen (or to other tools that collect such docs) since the definitions is private.

In `in-toto/securesystemslib` we made [`Key`](https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/compare/master...in-toto:securesystemslib:master#diff-ae2ca657fc834f3033da3502b45f73a8380f6a6cc2fde77fafb3715fa5237c65R12) an abstract interface to support multiple container formats and verification  implementations.

We made your traditional securesystemslib `Key` a subclass called [`SSlibKey`](https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/compare/master...in-toto:securesystemslib:master#diff-ae2ca657fc834f3033da3502b45f73a8380f6a6cc2fde77fafb3715fa5237c65R46) and added another subclass [`GPGKey`](https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/compare/master...in-toto:securesystemslib:master#diff-ae2ca657fc834f3033da3502b45f73a8380f6a6cc2fde77fafb3715fa5237c65R168) to allow independent attributes, and `verify` and `match_keyid` implementations.

The downside is that this makes deserialization harder. You'd have to call either `SSlibKey.from_dict` or `GPGKey.from_dict` based on the format of the passed dict, e.g. in python-tuf's `api.metadata.Root.from_dict`. But I think that's feasible. What do you think?

Errors in which `axis=XYZ` and `XYZ` is not a valid choice are handled all over the place in the codebase, but they mean the same thing to the user. I've been trying to consolidate their messages, so that they get to be more immediately recognizable, in part by using NumPy's `np.AxisError`, and by using the same message text.

```python
raise np.AxisError("axis={0} exceeds the depth of this array ({1})".format(axis, depth))
```

The `axis` is the `XYZ` that the user specifies (may be negative) and `depth` has a consistent meaning in all of these functions (fortunately!). In principle, one might think this calls for handling all axis errors in a subroutine, but then the last line number of the stack trace would be in that subroutine; to debug it, we'd have to remember to go one level up, and that's a little inconvenient. Also, _calling_ such a subroutine would be something we'd have to do consistently everywhere, and I don't see how it's any harder to just use consistent text. (The only argument would be if we planned on ever changing that text—I think we can safely ignore that possibility.)

So, please do change the exception class and message for this and any other "bad axis" exceptions.

I had considered doing `request`, but I wanted to patch whatever the lowest common denominator would be. It seems that no matter the method used, anything that needs to make an outbound call has to go through one of these two `send` methods.

For example, if the library added a `def multiple_requests()` method that doesn't use `request`, but still used `send`, then we wouldn't need to update anything in our integration.


There is always a balance to decide which/how deep to go for instrumentation. Since going too deep you might not have access to higher value contextual information from the public APIs (for example, is it still easy to access the headers or that it is a `GET` call?), vs instrumenting too high and needing to patch every single method that is exposed and chasing after constant API changes.

Can we discuss a little more around having `return SkipReason(...)` vs `return None` be significant for cursor updating logic?

I have some reservations (especially since this will be new behavior that only applies to this `@multi_asset_sensor`) so want to make sure we explore the full set of potential solutions.

For user-defined sensors, the cursor management logic is maintained by the user themselves, so it is reasonable that they call `context.update_cursor`.  For `@asset_sensor`, we fully manage the cursor management, so the user code doesn't have to do anything.  For these `@multi_asset_sensor`, I can completely see wanting to have some simplified cursor management, but maybe with a cleaner API.

Because we're creating a new context type, I can see us having some custom cursor management.  What do you think about something like `context.advance_cursor(AssetKey("asset_a"))` and `context.advance_all_cursors()` or something similar?  

They might need to work in concert with the event methods (e.g. `context.get_latest_event` / `context.get_all_events`).  For example, let's say that there have been 1000 materializations since the last cursor for a given sensor.  I could see wanting to do some amount of work for every single materialization (maybe there's a backlog), but I am fine with only processing 25 in this sensor evaluation.   Can I just get the earliest 25 materializations (without fetching all from the DB), advance the cursor for just that amount, and wait for the next sensor tick to process the remaining 975 materializations?

I think in order for this to work, `get_all_events` might need to return a generator so that under the hood we  can do batch fetching from the DB.

```
        pre_send_callback (list of method): The registered method will be called prior to each fuzz request.
                                            Default None.
```

This would be clearer if the argument names were plural. `pre_send_callbacks` instead of `pre_send_callback`, etc.

As for the default value situation, the way one can resolve this is to keep the default value at None, and add some code inside `__init__`:
```
        if pre_send_callbacks is None:
            pre_send_callbacks = []
        self._pre_send_methods = pre_send_callbacks
```

This makes the constructor more bulky, but has the significant advantage of giving maintainers one less thing to worry about -- `self._pres_send_methods` is always a list. No checks for None.

If you check `man 2 open` on Linux, you'll see that 2 forms of the `open` function exist, one that takes `mode`.  I found that in testing / experimentation with larger binaries, that if `mode` is removed, Manticore gets runtime errors when it hits that form of syscall.  Additionally, if its type is listed just as `int`, `typeguard` complains at times.

The syscall invocation mechanism in Manticore is actually pretty subtle; I wonder now if `mode` should _actually_ be defined via varargs... but I'll leave that investigation for future work.

- propose a change. If everyone agrees, go for it. Otherwise,
- try to convince any dissenters via the normal routes (on GitHub threads, mostly), if that works (possibly with some modifications), go for it. Otherwise,
- try to make your case with a SKIP.

In short, majority votes *never* decide a PR's merge status without a SKIP.

@dchigarev I investigated 3 approaches for computing `new_index`. Using `from_arrays` give ~2x speedup. Table and script for measurements are presented below:

|NROWS x NCOLS|Time approach 1, s|Time approach 2, s|Time approach 3, s|
|-------------------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
|5000 x 2| 0.05 | 0.03 | 0.02|
|5000 x 50| 0.57| 0.33|0.15|
|5000 x 100| 0.85| 0.66|0.27|
|100000 x 100| 13.06| 13.63|6.7|

<details>
<summary>Script</summary>

```python
import modin.pandas as pd
import pandas
import numpy as np
from timeit import default_timer as timer

NCOLS = 100
NROWS = 5000


def generate_columns(ncols):
    return pandas.Index([f"Col{i}" for i in range(ncols)])


def generate_index(nrows):
    l0 = ["a"] * (nrows // 2) + ["b"] * (nrows // 2)
    l1 = np.arange(0, nrows)
    l2 = np.arange(nrows, 2 * nrows)
    l3 = np.arange(2 * nrows, 3 * nrows)
    return pandas.MultiIndex.from_tuples(
        [(i, j, k, l) for i, j, k, l in zip(l0, l1, l2, l3)]
    )


# Calculate new multiindex using from_tuples
def gen_new_multiindex1(index, columns):
    return pandas.MultiIndex.from_tuples(
        [tuple([col] + list(tup)) for col in columns for tup in index]
    )


# Calculate new multiindex using from_tuples using `repeat`
def gen_new_multiindex2(index, columns):
    cols = columns.repeat(len(index))
    idx = list(index) * len(columns)
    return pandas.MultiIndex.from_tuples(
        [tuple([col] + list(tup)) for (col, tup) in zip(cols, idx)]
    )


# Calculate new multiindex using from_arrays
def gen_new_multiindex3(index, columns):
    idx = pandas.MultiIndex.from_tuples(list(index) * len(columns))
    cols = columns.repeat(len(index))
    idx_lvls = [idx.get_level_values(i) for i in range(idx.nlevels)]
    return pandas.MultiIndex.from_arrays([cols] + idx_lvls)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    columns = generate_columns(NCOLS)
    index = generate_index(NROWS)

    t1 = timer()
    res = repr(gen_new_multiindex1(index, columns))
    print("Approach #1:", "{:.2f}".format(timer() - t1), "s")

    t1 = timer()
    res = repr(gen_new_multiindex2(index, columns))
    print("Approach #2:", "{:.2f}".format(timer() - t1), "s")

    t1 = timer()
    res = repr(gen_new_multiindex3(index, columns))
    print("Approach #3:", "{:.2f}".format(timer() - t1), "s")
```
</details>

```suggestion
class GitRemoteRemoveHandler(GitHandler):
    """Handler for 'git remote remote <name>'."""
    
    @tornado.web.authenticated
    async def delete(self, path: str = "", name: str = ""):
        """DELETE request handler to remove a remote."""

            self.set_status(500)
```

- The code in this PR is explicit about this and ensures that we will have a single instance of each array which is shared by all users.

I believe the logic here is leading to test failures in CI, and this was actually a bug we encountered before where we were improperly setting the attributes. If the key is in self and not in dir, we need to call `__setitem__` and return so that we don't call the `object.__setattr__` at the bottom of the call. For example, in the failing test case we have this scenario where we are adding a new column as an attribute:
```python
def test___setattr__not_column():
    pandas_df = pandas.DataFrame([1, 2, 3])
    modin_df = pd.DataFrame([1, 2, 3])
    pandas_df.new_col = [4, 5, 6]
    modin_df.new_col = [4, 5, 6]
    df_equals(modin_df, pandas_df)
```

The current code as is will check if key is not in dir(self), which is true and will also see that key is not in self. We will print the warning, but we will also call `__setitem__` which is not what we want.

> Is it necessary to have another implementation for cached op? What does it take to make the original cached op thread safe? This makes the code hard to maintain.

Hi @eric-haibin-lin , the scope of this project was to allow for multi threaded inference with the cached op. This is used in the DJL project (https://github.com/awslabs/djl). There are two options for implementation : 1. either include all these changes with a different code path inside cached op code or 2. create a new implementation for thread safe cached op. Between the two, I find the latter to be easier to maintain. To make the existing cached op thread safe (as @rondogency pointed out), it would require training support, bulking support, multi context support, subgraph api support, dynamic shape support, ensuring that there are no perf regressions. At the very least, there is a lot of additional testing required. 

> Is there a plan of future works of thread_safe cached op?

For the future works of the thread_safe cached op, at this point we want to continue maintaining and addressing customer feedback for the current thread safe cached op version and address performance issues. 

> Do we plan to merge it back to original cached op?

I am not aware of a plan for this currently.

Some of this could be explored as a follow-up, but I'm wondering if we should more closely align our `collate_fn` semantics to the semantics for the Torch `DataLoader`'s semantics, to make porting more straightforward? https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#working-with-collate-fn

A few questions along these lines:
1. When automatic batching is disabled (in our case, `batch_size=None`), should `collate_fn` get the entire block, or one row at a time, like the Torch `DataLoader`'s `collate_fn`? I'd vote for the former since our API is more batch-centric (which I think is a good thing), but want to know if there are any other thoughts.
2. When automatic batching is enabled (in our case, `batch_size is not None`), should the default `collate_fn` follow the same pattern as `DataLoader`'s default `collate_fn`, i.e. preserve the input data's structure more faithfully rather than always converting to dict-of-ndarray batches (primarily for the simple block case). I'm again thinking that the dict-of-ndarray narrow waist is a good one that we should keep.

Both (1) and (2) are discrepancies that users might run into when porting from `DataLoader`, so I thought that they should be called out, especially since we're adding `collate_fn` explicitly to make that porting process easier.

Okay. Let's assume that the input shape is `(s1, s2, ..., sa - 1, sa, sa+1, ... , sb - 1, sb, sb + 1, ... sn)` where `a = axis1, b = axis2`. What would be the output shape? It would be `(s1, s2, ..., sa - 1, sa + 1, ..., sb - 1, sb + 1, ... sn, sd)` where `sd` is the size of the diagonal of a `sa x sb` matrix. Note that `s1, s2, ..., sa - 1`, `sa + 1, sa + 2, ..., sb - 1` and `sb + 1, sb + 2, ... sn` remain untouched (directly mapped), and for this reason we can merge them respectively. To remove `sa` and `sb` we can also merge them into segments `sa + 1, sa + 2, ..., sb - 1` and `sb + 1, sb + 2 , ... , sn` respectively, thus reshaping the original input into `(s1 * s2 * ... * sa - 1, sa * sa + 1 * ... * sb - 1,  sb * sb + 1 * ... * sn)`, and the corresponding output into `(s1 * s2 * ... * sa - 1, sa + 1 * sa + 2 * ... * sb - 1, sb + 1 * sb + 2 * ... * sn, sd)`. In this way, to get `output(x1, x2, x3, x4)`, we can simply find the index of `input(x1, x2, x3)` plus an offset `x4 * (stride1 + stride2)` where `stride1` and `stride2` are strides of `axis1` and `axis2` in the input respectively.

1. Requires-Dist: Need to only partially evaluate env markers for python_version / python_full_version (if --interpreter-constraint) but not anything else.
2. Wheel tags: Need to ignore these saving for the major / minor version part of the python tag if --interpreter-constraint.
3. Requires-Python: Need to ignore this / substitute info from --interpreter-constraint to select.

Starting from bytes on my system:

```console
$ du -sb /var/reana/users/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
345669  /var/reana/users/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
```

gives: 345669 bytes = 345.669 KB = 337.56 KiB... while we display 333.57 KB usage.

This means that the total number that we display is much closer to KiB units rather than KB units, as I suspected might be the case... Do we calculate KB/KiB always right? Or are we simply displaying wrong units?

To confirm/infirm this, I have made a quick helloworld test with a bigger extra data file:

```
$ ls -l bigfile
-rw-r--r-- 1 simko simko 838860800 Nov  4 18:56 bigfile
$ ls -lh bigfile
-rw-r--r-- 1 simko simko 800M Nov  4 18:56 bigfile
$ du -s -h /var/reana/users/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/workflows/4b40df8f-b776-416b-be42-e1f845fa0e68
801M    /var/reana/users/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/workflows/4b40df8f-b776-416b-be42-e1f845fa0e68
$ du -s --si /var/reana/users/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/workflows/4b40df8f-b776-416b-be42-e1f845fa0e68
839M    /var/reana/users/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/workflows/4b40df8f-b776-416b-be42-e1f845fa0e68
$ du -s -b /var/reana/users/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/workflows/4b40df8f-b776-416b-be42-e1f845fa0e68
838880491       /var/reana/users/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/workflows/4b40df8f-b776-416b-be42-e1f845fa0e68
```

... which means 838880491 bytes = 838.880491 MB = 800.02 MiB, while REANA displays 800.02 MB instead:

![2020-11-04-190358_710x107_scrot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/517546/98151472-8185ea00-1ed0-11eb-877c-6e75462d2faa.png)

... which seems to confirm the hypothesis.

There are two solutions in my eyes:

1. Keep displaying the number we have (800.02), but change units to KiB, MiB for both CLI and Web clients.
2. Keep displaying KB, MB units, but then with the proper 839M number.

This is going a bit further beyond the given PR at hand, but it would be good to complement the `-b` fix with fixing also units, so that we can fully close the original issue.

I don't think our rationale is to save disk space.
The main purposes is to:
1) Be able to fetch a SHA from a git server (reason for `refs/west`)
2) minimize when fetching is needed by keeping a reference to SHAs (west smart fetching)
  Also achieved `refs/west`
3) Avoid `refs/west` shows up, as example in `git log`

In general I still haven't made my mind if I am fine with making this annotation less strict. But just for the sake of argument - `Optional[Union[bool, int, float str]]` (extended https://github.com/zero323/pyspark-stubs/issues/275#issue-544150559) is something that is up for discussion as:

- There is reasonable justification for it - there are already `option` methods with `Bool`, `Double`, `Long`, `String`.
- These are rather unlikely to be extended and / or modified by the final user, so string representation is predictable.
- Smaller set of types is usually preferred, as it helps catching some naive mistakes.
- In case of unlikely event that this project is  merged with upstream and inlined, we really want to avoid `Any` (stubs don't validate against the annotated source, so it doesn't matter much at the moment).

Actually, we have a convention of using `"resources/<something>"` so this will work for users locally.

We've received a surprising number of bug reports from users running our samples and reporting errors like "I got a File Not Found cat.png!" so we [ship a folder of resources](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/cloud/speech/sample_resources.yaml) for [each library](https://github.com/googleapis/google-cloud-python/tree/master/speech/samples/resources) (_from the standard `gs://cloud-samples-data` bucket_). Usually this should be .csv's and the like, but there are 3 products which work on binary files ~> Vision (images), Speech (audio), Video (videos). Sample authors for those products should be very aware that any resources they add will end up in client library repos, so use sparingly.

Most products which accept `bytes` also accept GCS file paths which is generally favorable. So most samples tend to use GCS paths, but at least 1 sample per product should generally show developers how to Hello World call the API using an existing local file.

But to explain m current thinking:

Status quo is that `error_already_set` has the undocumented side effect*** of normalizing the Python exception. I see two "useful/good" options:

A: Document it, which means we're promising that it will stay that way.
B: Change the behavior to avoid the performance hit and document that.

I'm thinking A might be the way to go, but it also means that we're permanently accepting the performance hit.

Note that lazily building what() also leaves the object in a different state than before, potentially with similar consequences & surprises in the wild. It's a performance optimization with risks. Acceptable or not is a judgement call.

Anyway, let things be in double.  Things on the GPU are in float because it reduces GPU RAM consumption by 2x and the GPU hardware can do float arithmetic a lot faster than double arithmetic.  But that doesn't apply to the constants in this file, as they all run on the CPU.

I _think_ this is still a small issue, in that this call to `flush()` happens with the lock held, and if `schedule_flush_sync()` is unable to submit the flush to the request thread pool, then it will call `try_lock_and_do_flush_request` itself, which will fail to do anything because the lock is already held.  In theory `submit()` could throw an exception if the pool is shutting down or if it failed to create a new worker thread.  I'm not sure either of those conditions is really worth worrying about, and if the failure mode is just "we don't auto-flush the buffer in that case" that's probably fine -- it's just that the code is a little confused around that case.

I don't feel very strongly about it but if you wanted to address this, you might do something like check the buffer size inside the lock and set a local flag `doFlush = True` as needed; then check that flag and call `flush()` if necessary after releasing the lock.  That would introduce a possible race where multiple flush requests could get queued but that should be harmless as the ultimate flush logic will do nothing if there's nothing to flush.

As I said, I don't feel very strongly about it.  I'm mostly just noting it for posterity I guess.

Hmm, I think this may be because with `elem.value = -np.inf` the push and pop order of the markers is identical, while with `elem.value = image[index]` id depends on the markers value. 

My gut feeling is telling me that we don't want this, because now the output of `watershed(..., markers, ...)` also depends entirely on the given order of the `markers`. Additionally, if the user provides the minima themselves, they may get a different result compared to passing `markers=None` even though the docstring tells them that the result should be the same!

Going from the source cited in https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7071#discussion_r1271382091, I tend to prefer the following approach: set `elem.value = neg_inf` for all markers and sort the initial marker order by their image value. So [`utcoffset()` is also a Python builtin](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.utcoffset) which is overloaded by PyTz. The difference is that if `utcoffset` is called on a Python datetime then it will return the utcoffset of it's tz info called on itself. So in our example above we could have called:

    >>> dt.utcoffset()
    datetime.timedelta(-1, 59520)

Also FYI: there is an [inefficient loop in `rise_set_transit_ephem`](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/master/pvlib/solarposition.py#L545-L552) which uses slow `to_pydatetime()` and `utcoffset()`, but it's probably addressed in #588?

```
 *
 * Define as 1 to enable dynamic log level control.
 *
 * Note that the OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_LOG_LEVEL determines the log level at
 * compile time. The dynamic log level control (if enabled) only allows

 *

#endif
````

In my view, OpenThread CLI is a mirror/representation of OT public APIs, so all it should control is the OT itself.
So the CLI `ipmaddr` command should behave same way as `otIp6SubscribeMulticastAddress()` API.

OT core itself is not managing/creating any host/linux interface so should not be aware of it and/or control it in any way (same way that it would not be aware of any RTOS interfaces if used on an RTOS-based platform).

So if a user uses OT CLI command, it should be treated same as if public OT APIs are being called.

-------

The way I see it, is that syncing between host (interfaces) and OT is the job of a "host driver".

I would say "ot-daemon" is a host driver.

`wpantund` is also a  "host driver" and we have mechanisms in place in its design to ensure that it syncs addresses/routes/prefixes/etc between host and OT/NCP (it took some iterations and many bugs/issues with earlier approaches till we converged on this design in `wpantund`).

- In short, `wpantund` has a module which tracks all such entries (address/route/prefix/etc) and also tracks their origin (i.e. who added the entry, is it added by OT/NCP, or host interface, or user through other APIs, etc).
-  It has code that syncs all entries (e.g., if entry added by host interface push it to NCP, and vice versa). 
- This also allows us to protect entries, for example an address added by OT core cannot be removed by host/user. 
- Note that `wpantund` does not actually use the `address` callback feature we are discussing here and always gets the full list of addresses from NCP/OT.

On how to handle failures (when syncing entries), this is how `wpantund` behaves:
- We do have retry/wait mechanism if entry cannot be added on host/linux interface (we keep track of addresses to be added/removed and wait till host linux interface is ready/up to add/remove them)
- On OT/NCP side, we have the situation that we may run out of address space (as you mentioned there is a build-time configurable but fixed number of unicast/multicast addresses that is supported by OT).
  - `wpantund` behavior is that it would retry and then after some failures would assert (restart `wpantund`)
  - So while there are more addresses on host linux interface than what can be supported by the OT/NCP, `wpantund` would be in an assert loop (practically disabling Thread use).
  - While this is not perfect, choosing this behavior was very much intentional (in our internal projects) as it was the best way to indicate that there is something wrong with how the network is configured (too many addresses)

We considered other options: 
- If we ran out of address space on OT, just log it as error, and ignore it and let things proceed
   -  Not good, since user may want to use the added address and it won't work since it is not really added/supported on OT/NCP.
- If the address from host interface origin cannot be added on OT/NCP, go back and try to remove it on host interface     
    - Not good, since user adds the address on host interface and it succeeds but later it disappears.
- Provide API in driver to add/remove entries (addresses) and don't let user add/remove address on host linux interface - this would allow us to return error from API if out of space in OT. 
   - While I was fine with this approach, it is indeed limiting as it disallows user to treat Thread `wpan` interface like other interfaces (this was far from desirable by folks writing next layer (application) code).

For now, I think applying the rolling ball only to two dimensions does make sense. The most common use case for backround subtraction - that I know - is to correct for vignetting (images being brighter in the center than at the edges). Since this is a 2D effect, a 2D correction is totally sufficient.

I believe it would be great if it was possible to specify which dimensions to apply the ball to. For example, the last dimension is often the color dimension and in my case the first dimension is the time dimension.

The more I think about it, the more I get the impression that using a higher dimension ellipsoid could be interesting as well. For example, if you have a time series of moving objects, including the time dimension could help with finding the actual background level and avoid to remove signal. Imagine a bright cell that moves across the spot where you apply the ball. In the 2D case, the ball always "dips" into the center of the cell and removes part of the signal. In the 3D case, the ball will also take into account neighboring frames where the cell has moved away and the ball hits the correct background noise level. 

However, this feature would only be useful, if the radius of the ball could be specified separately for the spatial and for the temporal dimension, because the temporal resolution is not correlated with the spatial resolution.

Also for 3D images, in certain cases it could be an advantage to use a 3D ball. That way, the ball would again find the minimum noise level also in the 3rd dimension. This could be interesting for 3D structures that are large in the x and y dimensions but small in the z dimension.

tl/dr: Ideally, the dimensions that the ellipsoid/spheroid is applied to would be specified by a parameter. If only 2 dimensions are given, the behavior would be as it is now. If more dimensions are given, a higher dimension spheroid would be used.

Here is my understanding:
Section 3.2.6.3.1
>  CSL unsynchronized transmission and reception are outside the scope of the Thread specification.

This means `Thread 1.2` doesn't specify how should the CSL receiver handle unsynchronized CSL transmissions.

And Section 4.6.5.1.2 means that, if the application expects the CSL Receiver to process unsynchronized CSL transmissions, the Receiver should use a different channel. Then the next two paragraph said:
> During the attachment phase, the SSED Child MAY include the CslChannel TLV. If included,
> the Parent MUST perform the CSL transmissions on the channel indicated by the Child in the
> CslChannel TLV. In case of a successful CSL transmission, the SSED MUST send the IEEE
> 802.15.4 ACK on the channel indicated in the CslChannel TLV.
> If no CslChannel TLV was included by the Child during attachment, the Parent MUST
> perform CSL transmissions on the Thread Network channel.
So I think we should enable CSL Receiver to receive on another channel. Besides, in test harness, a case is certifying that.

1. use padding so that output is the same size as input
2. calculate the amount to slice by doing the math (a function of kernel size and dilation)
3. use `sym.infer_shape()` to infer shape of the output in hybridized mode.

I recommend option 2.

If there is an error, the response body will not be opened, so we should check for an error before closing the body.

Having a defer in there is just a best practice to help future-proof the code. Let's say something was added that read from the response body, it would need to return early if there was an error. Having the close in a defer just ensures that even if an error causes an early return, resources are still released. The small danger with not putting a defer block is that a future author may simply add something in before the `resp.Body.Close()` line thinking everything is ok, but if there is a possible return from the function introduced, the response body wouldn't get closed. This way, it is much harder to introduce such a resource leakage accidentally in a future modification of this routine.

> > Store is a tiny type that cheaply implements Clone
> 
> Ah cool, just like why you don't use references / borrowing for `i32`. Thanks!

There's a little more to this, actually - ideally we _would_ use references and borrowing here, because cloning this isn't nearly as cheap as copying an `i32`.

One of the functions we're going to end up passing this `Store` to is going to end up moving it onto another thread (to use it from a Future). If we borrowed a `Store` here, we'd need to clone the `Store` at the point that we move it onto that thread (because you can't borrow across that boundary).

As a rule, if you're going to borrow a value, and then unconditionally clone it, it's better to just take an owned value in the first place, because that's being up-front about the cost of the function. That said, when async/await happens, we can probably get rid of a bunch of those clones, because we should be able to borrow across futures boundaries.

For the curious: `tuple(some_object)` calls [`PySequence_Tuple(some_object)`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2fe408497e6838b6fb761e42e8f8ffc70bd6f3b1/Objects/abstract.c#L1908); if the `some_object` argument is exactly a `list`, we wind up in [`PyList_AsTuple(some_object)`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2fe408497e6838b6fb761e42e8f8ffc70bd6f3b1/Objects/listobject.c#L2485), which in turn passes a direct reference to the list's C-level `PyObject*` array back to [`_PyTuple_FromArray`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2fe408497e6838b6fb761e42e8f8ffc70bd6f3b1/Objects/tupleobject.c#L414). That function just does some C pointer walking to initialize the new new tuple's C array.

Every other path through `PySequence_Tuple` (except for the trivial case of `tuple(other_tuple)` resort to the much more expensive case of using generic `iter(some_object)` and `next()` calls to populate the tuple.

Yes, you can use `with nogil` in a Cython function defined with `def`.  Here, the double `nogil` is because `cpdef` generates both the Python wrapper and the pure C-function - the C-function uses the declaration `nogil`, while the Python wrapper makes use of the `with nogil`. 

I generally use `cpdef` when reasonable, even if `def` would suffice at the time, to expose future possibilities.  We don't use it here right now, in this PR, but for the price of a function declaration we ship the ability for someone to be much faster if they want to call this from pure C.  And it's fast enough that it might be useful for real-time work, so I'm trying to be as sensitive to that as possible.

That's a good question! There are two ways to make projective (Jacobian) coordinates.
- (X / Z, Y / Z)
- (X / Z^2, Y / Z^3)

As the `py_ecc` library uses `(X / Z, Y / Z)` hence the final output must be in that form. Thus, I've had to diverge from that paper such that we have end up with our projective form.

If we were to switch to `(X / Z^2, Y / Z^3)` we would have to re-write most of the library. The `(X / Z^2, Y / Z^3)` form is a little slower but has some protection against certain side channel attacks.

This would be a really *bad* idea.  Setting the optional parameter to `None` **is** the pattern to indicate that nothing has been passed.

The reason is that Python passes parameters by reference, if if the default for a parameter is a boxed (i.e. a reference to a type) like `Dict` or `List` then *only on the first call* is the default param initialised.  Thus, it acts like a local cache and is the same dictionary on each invocation:

```
$ ipython3
Python 3.8.5 (default, Jan 27 2021, 15:41:15)
Type 'copyright', 'credits' or 'license' for more information
IPython 7.13.0 -- An enhanced Interactive Python. Type '?' for help.

In [1]: def a(x, y={}):
   ...:     y[x] = 1
   ...:     print(y)
   ...:

In [2]: a('hello')
{'hello': 1}

In [3]: a('goodbye')
{'hello': 1, 'goodbye': 1}
```

So this is that correct pattern.

I'd recommend switching to a `push` `pop` approach to changing directories.  Simply changing directories in the middle of a build process can have unexpected consequences that are difficult to debug.  The `push` `pop` approach ensures the working directory remains unchanged for all subsequent processes.

Example of `push` `pop` approach:
```
pushd ${SCRIPT_PATH} >/dev/null

# Do some work in ${SCRIPT_PATH} 

popd >/dev/null
```

The `safe_copy` tries to use hard linking, and when that succeeds, you can get a symlink out the other side if `f` went in as a symlink (hardlink of a symlink on linux is a symlink). Wrapping the LHS with an os.path.realpath fixes and lets --spread be used over in Pants where it nets a ~23x space savings vs master when running:
```console
rm -rf ~/.cache/pants/lmdb_store
./pants fmt lint typecheck test build-support:: src:: tests::
``` 
I found:
```console
du -sb lmdb_store.old lmdb_store.main lmdb_store.spread
1623506944	lmdb_store.old
1614954496	lmdb_store.main
1423679488	lmdb_store.spread
```
So that's
old: 1623506944
PEX_PATH transitive: 1614954496 (-8552448) ~8MB saved.
spread: 1423679488 (-199827456) ~200MB saved, ~23x improvement.

IIUC the local lmdb savings should directly map to remote CAS savings / hit ratios.

I understand that the choice of offering `inplace` support won't make everyone happy. The reason I decided to support it is:
- It is more performant in terms of memory and speed.
- Most the tensor-only transforms on torchvision typically support `inplace` operations (see `Normalize` and `RandomErasing`). 
- The ClassyVision version of this method [supported](https://github.com/facebookresearch/ClassyVision/blob/8e3d7587e4102cf308ee77498fa0ff395058bc1b/classy_vision/dataset/transforms/mixup.py#L284) inplace operations and thus to ensure we are equally performant we should support it as well.

This class looks a lot like overkill, but it is actually not:

1. Since `lambda`'s are "not welcome" in datapipes that take a callable (a warning will be thrown), we often need to use `functools.partial(..., config=config)`. To do that `config` has to be hashable. Hence, we implement the `__hash__` and `__eq__` methods and fail in case `__getitem__` or `__delitem__` are invoked.
2. For convenience it is much nicer to use a `DatasetConfig` as namespace as a dictionary, so we additionally implement `__getattr__`, `__setattr__`, and `__delattr__`.

```suggestion
When bots start to get considerably large, the amount of events they have to deal with can start to become problematic.

At high user and guild counts the incoming messages, typing events, and status updates can start to climb to being as frequent as hundreds per second.

To help deal with this large amount of traffic, Discord supports **sharding**, a feature where your bot can split its guilds amongst separate connections, reducing the amount of data each individual connection has to handle.

This not only helps Discord by reducing how much data they need to direct to one place, but also helps us, as we can split our bot's overall work across different connections, environments, or even entirely different machines. 

Discord recommends using sharding once you get over 1,000 guilds, and once you reach beyond 2,500 guilds, it becomes a requirement.

Let's discuss what options we have for setting up sharding within discord.py.
```

For cases where we are searching within a list/array/container for an entry (while we can use `VerifyOrExit` and it would be fewer lines) the preferred approach is `"if (entry satisfies certain conditions) then exit"` which reads much more clearly (and therefore less chance of a mistake), compared to `"verify that entry does not satisfy conditions or exit"`.

`VerifyOrExit` suits well for checking and verifying that some conditions are true before continuing on with next lines of code and logic.

In general, I personally like the syntax sugars and using fewer lines wherever possible, but in this case, I also do favor readability of the style "if (check) then exit" approach.

Stuck on type-hinting this function signature, specifically the return type.

This function can return a `Node`, `None`, or `REMOVE`. It turns out the GraphQL library [sets](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/master/src/graphql/language/visitor.py#L34) `REMOVE` to the Python literal `Ellipsis`, not `None`-- I think PyCharm got confused because there's a stub file that sets `Ellipsis` to `None`, but when this code actually runs `Ellipsis != None`.

The current error says that this type-hint isn't valid and `note: Suggestion: use type[...] instead of type(...)`.

* type[ellipsis] and type[type(Ellipsis)] don't work because of the square brackets.

* Although `type(Ellipsis)` is the class `ellipsis`, adding `ellipsis` to the union in place of `type(REMOVE)` makes both tests and linter to fail because `ellipsis` is undefined.

* Replacing `type(REMOVE)` with `Type[type(REMOVE)]` also makes the linter fail and produces a link to [this documentation](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/common_issues.html#variables-vs-type-aliases). It seems unrelated to what the type hint we're trying to do here because I a) haven't been able to find the `ellipsis` class and b) it makes more sense to depend on the type of `REMOVE` as much as possible rather than rely on the fact that it's set to `Ellipsis`.

Another idea: subclass the `TracebackException` and use `_is_sacred_frame` while creating the stack summary. I did a quick check in a notebook, but this has the disadvantage of copying the whole `TracebackException.__init__`:
```python
def _is_sacred_frame(frame):
    return frame.f_globals["__name__"].split(".")[0] == "sacred"

def walk_tb_filtered(frame_gen):
    for tb_frame, tb_lineno in tb.walk_tb(frame_gen):
        if not _is_sacred_frame(tb_frame):
            yield tb_frame, tb_lineno

class FilteredTracebackException(tb.TracebackException):
    def __init__(self, exc_type, exc_value, exc_traceback, *, limit=None,
            lookup_lines=True, capture_locals=False, _seen=None):
        if _seen is None:
            _seen = set()
        _seen.add(id(exc_value))
        if (exc_value and exc_value.__cause__ is not None
            and id(exc_value.__cause__) not in _seen):
            cause = TracebackException(
                type(exc_value.__cause__),
                exc_value.__cause__,
                exc_value.__cause__.__traceback__,
                limit=limit,
                lookup_lines=False,
                capture_locals=capture_locals,
                _seen=_seen)
        else:
            cause = None
        if (exc_value and exc_value.__context__ is not None
            and id(exc_value.__context__) not in _seen):
            context = TracebackException(
                type(exc_value.__context__),
                exc_value.__context__,
                exc_value.__context__.__traceback__,
                limit=limit,
                lookup_lines=False,
                capture_locals=capture_locals,
                _seen=_seen)
        else:
            context = None
        self.exc_traceback = exc_traceback
        self.__cause__ = cause
        self.__context__ = context
        self.__suppress_context__ = \
            exc_value.__suppress_context__ if exc_value else False
        # This here filters the traceback
        self.stack = tb.StackSummary.extract(
            walk_tb_filtered(exc_traceback), limit=limit, lookup_lines=lookup_lines,
            capture_locals=capture_locals)
        self.exc_type = exc_type
        self._str = tb._some_str(exc_value)
        if exc_type and issubclass(exc_type, SyntaxError):
            # Handle SyntaxError's specially
            self.filename = exc_value.filename
            self.lineno = str(exc_value.lineno)
            self.text = exc_value.text
            self.offset = exc_value.offset
            self.msg = exc_value.msg
        if lookup_lines:
            self._load_lines()

try:
    c()
except:
    exc_type, exc_value, exc_traceback = sys.exc_info()
    tb_exception = FilteredTracebackException(
        exc_type, exc_value, exc_traceback, limit=None
    )
    
print(''.join(tb_exception.format()))
```

This test is intentionally commented out for few reasons. The goal of this test was to run the same commands with local context turned ON. However, the major problem this test was causing is :
1. The test should ideally run the commands and check for successful execution of the command **OR** it should validate the output of the commands. Currently, it is missing out on both. 
2. Since the tests execute in parallel and this test **turns on** the local context, it is inconsistently impacting the other tests caused by a race condition. If the local-context tests get kicked off before other tests, all commands in other tests is starting to pick up the values from local context since local context is turned on in the environment due to this  test. This leads to the failure of other live tests.

With the introduction of minute, hour, and day offsets, an issue in this function became apparent. Since the hour/minute offset is truncated from the partition key, returning `TimeWindow(start, next(iterator))` would return an incorrect window. 

For example, if I had daily partition with offset of 4:15, then my partition key is still `2022-03-28` and that means that `2022-03-28-00:00` would be set as the beginning of the returned time window.

Returning TimeWindow(next(iterator), next(iterator)) instead will return the first partition starting on or after `partition_key`.

@ibnesayeed let us describe the calls sequence:

```python
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

seemingly calls `main()`, but _actually_ calls `wrapper(*args, **kwargs)` (see the implementation of `exception_logger`).

Then, `wrapper` calls the original `main()` inside a `try - except` block.

Now what happens if an unhandled exception pops out somewhere in `main()` or one of the functions it calls? Being unhandled, that exception pops up through the call stack, reaches `main()`, passes it and arrives at `wrapper()`.

The execution of `main()` at this point is already interrupted by a propagating exception. `wrapper()` now can either reraise the exception or just log it - that does not matter because, when its `except` clause is done its work, `wrapper()` function returns and the process stops.

At least it should if everything works as I believe it does.

We believe it's not efficient to directly use MAs in `Child` and Netif. For example, if one child subscribes to `ff04::1`, the parent will have to traverse all addresses of all children (there might be hundreds of them) to determine if this is a newly subscribed address that needs a MLR.req. Same happens when a Child unsubscribes to a address.

To the opposite, we considered moving multicast addresses from Netif and Child to Multicast Table because `Multicast Table` is much more efficient in memory footprints if many Children are subscribing the same multicast address, which we expect to be a common case. 

I prefer to enhance Child in a separate PR.

The current thunk system uses registerNatives to do all the name binding rather than the expected Java_ natives load mechanism.  It was required because of the rather strange way that JPype was booted up with loading a jar from the inside of shared library.  The usual way that a Java library is loading is to name all the entry points with a Java name mangling convention and then naming the so the correct way so that Java loads it automatically.  Unfortunately, we have to name our shared library to match Python naming so we have to manuallyloading your native library with a  System.load.  

When I started the porting Python from Java launch I suddenly needed 50 native entry points.  It is simply a huge burden to manually connect all the natives (which is not checked).   To make it manageable a switch was required.  We already had most of the methods named correctly, so the process was just rename the entry points, remove the register natives calls, and then make the final linkage.

The new load sequence is as follows.  I flipped the process around, rather than building a jar and compiling it into the shared library, instead we build a jar and call it a "Python" extension so it gets installed as the same place as _jpype.  We then have the module find its file location and pass it to the start routine so that the native sections are loaded in mass using System.load. 

The same mechanism is used to start Python from Java.  In that case we know where the jar is and we need to get our Python module.  

Sorry for not explaining it here.   Technically I am moving from our very unconventional loading method to a more standard one, but it does need some explanation as the process is still depending on two pieces that have naming conflicts.  Python requires the file to be named one way, Java requires another, so I have to do the last part of the link manually here.

```py
def odd_or_none(value: int) -> int | None:

    return value
```

In this example we explicitly return `None` as the result of the function is going to be used somewhere and `None` would mean something to that logic.

I intended the bool return values mostly for the parser to know if there's any missing items (keys or values) so it wouldn't iterate to the next item assuming it was a new key. Unknown key/value pairs are parseable (simply ignored). 

However, I agree with you that we should return false here for when we add in the warning messages that'll let the users know which keys weren't parsed (with the verbosity flag), so I did add the `else return false;` in for this and `setFontInfoKey`. 

Now, once it returns the false from setXMLFileKey to its caller, it iterates to the cur->next, which is the keyValue (that wasn't set because it was unknown), and then it realizes it's not a key and skips it again to get to the next key/value pair. So returning false for unknown key/value still functions as expected for this addition.

`keyValueValid` only checks if the key value is parsable, so I wouldn't add the assert. I renamed `keyValueValid` to `keyValueParseable` to clarify its purpose.

I think I'd rather keep the `_is_builtins_object` as it is, so that it does what it says on the tin (tells you whether the node represents `builtins.object` or not). This patch would change the function so that it tells you whether the node represents `builtins.object` or this other symbol that's an alias for `builtins.object` or not. That's slightly different (`_typeshed.Unused` has a different semantic meaning to the human reader, even if it's all the same to a type checker), and might impact our ability to reuse the `_is_builtins_object` function in the future, if we ever add more checks that to test whether a node represents `builtins.object` or not.

How about we do something like this, instead?

```python
_is_builtins_object = partial(_is_object, name="object", from_={"builtins"})
_is_Unused = partial(_is_object, name="Unused", from_={"_typeshed"})

def _is_object_or_Unused(node: ast.expr | None) -> bool:
    return _is_builtins_object(node) or _is_Unused(node)
```

And then we use `_is_object_or_Unused` in Y036 instead of `_is_builtins_object`?

This is not an exhaustive comment, but here are some links:

https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logrecord-objects:

> msg – The event description message, possibly a format string with placeholders for variable data.

https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.debug:

> The msg is the message format string, and the args are the arguments which are merged into msg using the string formatting operator.

https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0282/:

> In particular, localization and formatting (which are relatively expensive) are deferred until the Handler requests them.

https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html#logging-variable-data:

> To log variable data, use a format string for the event description message and append the variable data as arguments.

https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html#loggers:

> The message is actually a format string, which may contain the standard string substitution syntax of %s, %d, %f, and so on. The rest of their arguments is a list of objects that correspond with the substitution fields in the message.

https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html#optimization:

Yea, this is a very fundamental question that we're going to want to experiment with the answer to.

As it stands with the `EnvironmentName` in most (but not all: only portions which actually consume it indirectly via a `Platform`/`Process`/option, etc) portions of the graph, there is a high likelihood of redundant work unless some portions of the graph are pinned back to the current environment.

----

My initial feeling (with not much evidence) is that we're going to want to always pin all `Dependencies` computation (`TransitiveTargets`/`CoarsenedTargets`/`DependenciesRequest`/etc) to the `__local__` environment.

The reasoning for that is that we already know that it is working for our existing users: even if they deploy to production using a different platform, their users currently have enough set up in their local environments (be it Linux or macOS) to allow for dependencies to be computed... and probably also for `check`/`lint`/etc to run.

While we could definitely enable new use cases by running the entire graph in the docker environment (i.e. even the "root" environment computed in #16786 would be a docker environment), those are the use cases which users currently satisfy by running the entire Pants process inside docker. So that would be a nice to have, but less important than just ensuring that your _**runtime**_ environment is a particular platform.

So yea: we initially care primarily about controlling the _runtime_ environment. Given that, dependency computation seems like it can/should be pinned to `__local__`, to allow it to be shared across all environments. But having said that: I _think_ that avoiding re-computing dependencies in a bunch of environments is primarily a performance concern rather than a correctness one (fairly unsure here), so I think that opening an issue to audit the impact in situations where we're running tests across multiple environments might be fine (rather than worrying about it in this PR).

mypy errors on overloaded signatures that overlap and have incompatible return types like the iterable ones do in this case because the type variable can match anything including the string list and tuples and the types of the dict returns are fixed and incompatible. More info [here](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/more_types.html#type-checking-the-variants).

This could yield wrong types if the user's types are not narrow enough. For example we can port the example in the mypy docs to this case:
```py
import pydash

x: t.List[object] = [('a', 'b'), ('c', 'd')]  # this is right because tuple is an object too

reveal_type(to_dict(x))  # Revealed type is "builtins.dict[builtins.int, builtins.object]"
```

This is wrong, it matches the last overload because they overlap.

Unfortunately there is no way to make a type excluding other types in the python type system currently so I don't think there is a more correct way to type this. We could remove the `Iterable[T]` case to be more on the safe side so that it falls back to the last case and we still get a `Dict` type without the key and value types. 


> And I guess even with that ignore, the typing still gets applied?

And yes otherwise, the types still get applied, this just ignores the check of the overload itself, but when using it, it will still be applied as is validated in the type tests I added

Yeah I was reluctant to change project config as part of an unrelated PR, but I explain in the commit why it seems necessary.

Here's a lot more detail:

If I take a fresh checkout of Dagster and hit "save" in vscode[1] on a Python file, I get the following error:

> [ERROR 2022-1-23 15:25:57.310]:     raise MissingSection(import_module=import_from, section=placed_module)
isort.exceptions.MissingSection: Found dagster.core.instance import while parsing, but FIRSTPARTY was not included in the `sections` setting of your config. Please add it before continuing
See https://pycqa.github.io/isort/#custom-sections-and-ordering for more info.

The isort documentation says:

> You can change the section order with sections option from the default of:
>
>     FUTURE,STDLIB,THIRDPARTY,FIRSTPARTY,LOCALFOLDER
>
> to your preference (if defined, omitting a default section may cause errors)...

(Note that this commit sets the config to the above default setting for isort)

Upon further research I discovered that Dagster requires isort version 4. If I upgrade to isort 5 within the project and run it from the commandline I get the above error.

So:

1. that addition indeed isn't necessary now, but will be whenever you upgrade to isort 5
2. it's broken by default currently for vscode users, as the vscode Python extension bundles isort 5
3. best I can tell, the addition of `FIRSTPARTY` to the isort config doesn't change behavior in version 4, but allows it to work for vscode users and isort 5

I did find an example file (`python_modules/dagster/dagster/cli/pipeline.py`) that behaves differently with that setting under isort 5 than under isort 4. Currently the imports in that file look like:

```python
import click
import pendulum
import yaml
from dagster import PipelineDefinition
...
from dagster.utils.interrupts import capture_interrupts
from tabulate import tabulate

from .config_scaffolder import scaffold_pipeline_config
```

isort 4 with that change still sorts the imports the same. Under isort 5, with the (required) addition of `FIRSTPARTY` sorts it as follows:

```python
import click
import pendulum
import yaml
from tabulate import tabulate

from dagster import PipelineDefinition
...
from dagster.utils.interrupts import capture_interrupts

from .config_scaffolder import scaffold_pipeline_config
```

That's actually *correct* because `tabulate` is a third-party module. isort 4 is sorting imports incorrectly!

I'll back out that change to the isort config, but I recommend updating to isort 5.

[1] With the Python extension and "organize imports on save" in settings.json:

```json
    "[python]": {
        "editor.codeActionsOnSave": {
            "source.organizeImports": true
        }
    },
```

Yes, there is a way to say in typing that something has a specific value, via the `Literal` type: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0586.

However, that type was only added to the standard library in Python 3.8, so I don't think it should be used in `lightgbm`.

```text
docker run -t python:3.7-slim python -c "from typing import Literal"
```

> Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: cannot import name 'Literal' from 'typing' (/usr/local/lib/python3.7/typing.py)

I think a better solution would be to leave the hint as `Dict[str, Any]`, and add to the docstring that anything in that dictionary is going to be removed by `lightgbm`.

That's still a correct documentation of what the eventual value will be. Using an inner type in containers like `Dict[]`, `List[]`, etc. doesn't necessarily imply that they'll be initialized with any contents.

e.g. this pattern is valid and common:

```python
def is_in_list(number: int, numbers: List[int]) -> bool:
    return number in numbers
```

even though that function might be called with an empty list, like `is_in_list(5, [])`

> Dans le cas du simulateur d'aides pour les jeunes, au moins dans un premier temps, on en va pas une date de début et de fin, mais seulement si il y a une volonté de la part du jeune de partir à l'étranger. On va donc devoir donner des valeurs par défaut qui vont bien pour que ces dates ne soient pas limitantes.

Je crois que c'est justement la différence entre OpenFisca, qui offre un _modèle de la législation_, et les réutilisations qui peuvent se permettre de faire des hypothèses spécifiques à leurs cas d'usage. Ça ne me choque pas du tout que la loi ne sache parler qu'en termes de dates de début et de fin d'un séjour à l'étranger, et qu'une application à destination des jeunes parle en termes de volonté de partir à l'étranger 🙂 et que techniquement, ça fasse porter une part de la charge à l'application en question qui va créer des situations théoriques représentant les cas associés, avec sa connaissance des hypothèses raisonnables à appliquer.

En ce qui concerne la création de variables intermédiaires, pour ma part, bien au contraire, l'expérience de cette PR pèse dans l'autre sens. Le niveau de découpage élevé dans le modèle de la bourse sur critères sociaux rend en effet complexe sa réutilisation : pour l'aide fournie aux personnes inscrites dans les formations labellisées par la Grande École du numérique, les calculs sont les mêmes que ceux de la bourse sur critères sociaux, avec simplement des conditions supplémentaires de ressources. Les problèmes rencontrés sont les suivants :

1. Impossibilité de reproduire les tests : les tests suivent une approche _white box_ et dépendent donc de la connaissance de toutes les variables intermédiaires. Je ne peux pas changer la _situation_ à proprement parler, je dois définir des variables intermédiaires dont je n'ai pas connaissance.
2. Difficulté à identifier les variables à définir : je dois lire le contenu des formules pour déterminer lesquelles peuvent être réutilisées vs celles qui ne sont que des résultats de calculs intermédiaires.
3. Espaces de nommages restreints : certaines variables d'entrée sont communes à d'autres dispositifs, mais sont préfixées de `bourse_critères_sociaux` sans raison apparente. Par exemple, la distance entre le domicile familial et le lieu d'études. Certes, cette variable est introduite pour le calcul de la bourse sur critères sociaux, mais elle décrit un état de fait qui n'est pas spécifique à ce calcul. Pour la réutiliser, je dois la renommer, ce qui implique un changement non rétro-compatible.
